question: Why can't you use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * together with credentials: true?

options.text: The CORS spec forbids wildcards with credentials -- the browser requires a specific origin for cookie security
Wildcard origin doesn't work in Chrome
options.explanation: Allow-Origin: * works in all browsers, but only without credentials. With credentials: true, a specific origin is needed -- this is a CORS spec requirement, not a browser limitation

options.text: Cookies aren't sent with wildcards due to a fetch API bug
options.explanation: This isn't a bug but an intentional security restriction. If wildcards worked with cookies, any site could send requests with user cookies to your API
Wildcard origin works with HTTPS. The restriction is about security: with credentials: true, the server must explicitly specify a trusted origin to prevent CSRF

question: Why send X-RateLimit-Reset (Unix timestamp) in rate limiting headers?

options.text: The client can calculate when to send the next request and implement exponential backoff or waiting
It's an HTTP standard, without it the request will be rejected
options.explanation: X-RateLimit-* headers aren't an HTTP standard -- they're a convention. The request won't be rejected without this header, but the client won't be able to intelligently plan retries

options.text: The browser automatically retries the request after Reset
options.explanation: The browser doesn't handle X-RateLimit-Reset automatically. It's information for client code to implement retry logic with proper timing

options.text: Without this header, CDN won't cache the response
options.explanation: CDN caching depends on Cache-Control, not X-RateLimit-Reset. Rate limit headers are client information about limits, not for caching

question: How does Token Bucket differ from Fixed Window for rate limiting?

options.text: Token Bucket allows burst traffic (series of requests), gradually refilling tokens, while Fixed Window counts in rigid windows
Token Bucket is faster because it doesn't store request history
options.explanation: Fixed Window also doesn't store history -- just a counter. The difference is behavior: Token Bucket allows burst up to maxTokens, then limits to refillRate

options.text: Fixed Window is more accurate, Token Bucket is approximate
options.explanation: Fixed Window is less accurate due to window boundary issues (2x burst). Token Bucket provides smoother limiting with support for legitimate burst patterns

options.text: Token Bucket only works with Redis, Fixed Window works in-memory
options.explanation: Both algorithms can be implemented in-memory or in Redis. The difference is the model: Token Bucket -- tokens + refill, Fixed Window -- counter + reset

question: When should the server return 304 Not Modified?

options.text: When the client sent If-None-Match with an ETag matching the current one -- data hasn't changed, no body needed
When the client repeats the same request within max-age
options.explanation: Within max-age, the browser uses the cache and doesn't make a server request at all. 304 is returned for conditional requests with If-None-Match or If-Modified-Since
Idempotency isn't related to 304. The server returns 304 only when the client sends an ETag (If-None-Match) or date (If-Modified-Since), and data hasn't changed

options.text: Automatically for all GET requests after the first one
options.explanation: 304 isn't automatic -- the client must send conditional headers (If-None-Match/If-Modified-Since). Without them, the server returns a full 200 response
